dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious/fainting episode at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been feeling super dizzy and weak for the last three days. It got worse today with some nausea and diarrhea.
Ma'am, can you tell me where we are right now to check your alertness?
We're in - that's the only part I can recall.
Great. Can you tell me what day it is today?
I think it's the 24th. Not sure.
Can you open both of your eyes fully for me?
Alright, go ahead.
What is your name?
My name is Clara.
Can you point to your left and then your right?
Okay, left with left, right with right. Got it. (She points as directed.)
Your airway is clear and you're breathing on your own. Any trouble opening your mouth?
Nope, I can breathe fine.
I'm going to check your breathing. Is that okay with you?
Can you take a deep breath for me?
Respirations are 20 - within normal range.
SpO2 is 99%. Also in the normal range.
Radial pulse is weak but regular. I'll have my partner check your blood pressure.
Systolic is 67 and diastolic is 49. That's low but stable.
You mentioned feeling weak - have you experienced any malaise with it?
Yes, I feel like I'm not quite myself - sluggish and tired.
When you mentioned nausea, did you have any blood in your stool or significant fluid loss? When did the nausea start today?
No blood that I recall. Nausea started in the morning. Diarrhea came on in the evening.
Do you have any allergies?
No known allergies.
Are you on any medications, including over-the-counter or supplements?
I take Lisinopril for high blood pressure every morning and Percocet for pain if needed.
What is your past medical history besides your recent cancer diagnoses?
I've had high blood pressure, kidney stones, and anxiety that's under control now.
I noticed you haven't mentioned any pain. Do you have any discomfort or pressure right now?
No pain at all - just fatigue and weakness.
I'll take a quick look at your skin for texture and abnormalities - if that's okay with you.
Sure.
Skin is dry, warm, and normal in color - no swelling or rashes.
I'll do a quick inspection of your ears, nose, and throat - anything strange or unusual?
No signs of drainage or other abnormalities noted.
I'm going to listen to your lungs with a stethoscope - do you mind?
Clara, you okay with this?
Yeah, go ahead.
Let me check your blood sugar. I just need a quick drop of blood.
Go ahead.
Blood glucose is 127 mg/dL - that's normal.
Not surprised. I've been eating less lately.
I'll just recheck your pressure again to be sure.
Blood pressure is 62/48 - it's slightly improved but still low.
Pulse is 74 - stable.
Oriented to person, place, time, and event - GCS is 15 (E4;V5;M6). Great response.
Your initial pulse was 80 when we first arrived, but it's now 74 and remains stable.
I'll start an IV for fluids now. It's a 20 gauge at the left forearm.
Breathing is still 16 per minute and non-labored - all within normal range.
SpO2 is 98% - still good.
I'll place you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor now. Are you okay with that?
Whatever helps.
You're in sinus rhythm.
Based on low systolic blood pressure and the low perfusion range, we're stabilizing you under the hypotension and shock protocol.
After administering fluids, systolic went up to 95/39 - later dropped to 64/52 - we still need to monitor closely.
We rechecked your BP and found it to be 55/33 - slightly lower now.
Repeat 12-lead ECG done - classic signs of inferoapical infarct but no STEMI found.
SpO2 rechecked - 96%. Good oxygenation.
You're being taken to VCU for a full evaluation. They have a trauma team ready for you.
We'll help you walk from the couch to the stretcher. Is that okay?
Yes, I can walk there.
We'll guide you slowly - just walk at your pace.
Here we go, Clara. You're doing well.
We placed you in a comfortable position with two blankets to keep you warm and supported.
We're now securing you to the stretcher to prepare for transport - tell us if there's any pain or discomfort.
Handing over care to the trauma team at VCU Health - we have a 65-year-old female with a history of hypertension and malignancies presenting with hypotension, dizziness, and nausea.
We completed field 12-lead EKGs with findings of inferoapical infarct seen on initial and repeat EKGs; still in sinus rhythm with no STEMI.
She received 800 mL of normal saline in two boluses under hypovolemia protocol.
Your blood pressure has dropped again - this time it's 65/46. We'll continue monitoring.
Clara is still AAOx4 - no change in mental status.
Recheck the secured position - five point restraints applied, she is resting comfortably with blankets.
She was walked into the stretcher with a steady gait and minimal assistance needed.
She was transported in a position that ensured accessibility to emergency interventions if needed.
Orion seat assigned with no anticipated transport trauma risks.
Keep the IV in place, stay on the monitor, and glide smoothly is our transit plan.
Wheels up - and chair ready for secure movement into the back of the ambulance. Ready to roll.
Rolling into the rear, and destination locking in.
Ambulance set to departure with all vitals and assessments up to date.
Let's maintain the current status and monitor during transport.
On route - in communication with ER and protocols initiated as per ePCR.
ED trauma bay set to receive - transfer will include an RAA stretcher move with pull sheet protocol.
All documentation and medication logs are clean and in the report.
Care has been reported, and full handoff to trauma team at their trauma bay is ready.
She has ongoing care needs and is not actively declining as of the latest reassessment.
She's becoming a non-critical yet resource-heavy transfer. All done.
No signs of thorax or lung masses or cancers noted on inspection.
You have a history of thyroid and lung or bronchus cancer, as mentioned. That complicates your hypotension case.
